content.text: Post-training quantization (PTQ) reduces the numerical precision of a neural network's weights and activations after training is complete — without modifying the training procedure or recomputing gradients. A 70B-parameter model stored in bfloat16 (2 bytes per parameter) occupies ~140 GB; quantized to INT4 (0.5 bytes), it fits in 35 GB — well within a single A100 or two consumer RTX 4090s. PTQ is the dominant technique for deploying large language models because it requires only a small calibration dataset and a few minutes of computation, compared to the weeks of training needed for quantization-aware training (QAT).
Quantization maps a continuous floating-point value to a discrete integer representation. For a weight tensor W stored in FP32, the mapping to signed INT8 is:
During inference, the integer weights are dequantized back to floating-point for the matrix multiply:

The difference W - W_hat is quantization error (also called quantization noise). PTQ minimises this error by computing the scale s carefully, using statistics collected over a small calibration set rather than the full training corpus.

attrs.alt: PTQ pipeline: pretrained FP32 model → calibration → scale/zero-point computation → quantized INT8/INT4 model. Three PTQ strategies shown: GPTQ (layer-wise Hessian), SmoothQuant (activation migration), AWQ (salient channel protection).

content.text: Calibration is the process of measuring the range of activations and weights in the model to determine the optimal scale factors. Unlike full training, calibration uses only 128–512 unlabelled samples from the target domain and runs in minutes on a single GPU:

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_dataset
import torch

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3-8B", torch_dtype=torch.float16).cuda()
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3-8B")

# Calibration dataset: 512 random samples from C4
calib_data = load_dataset("allenai/c4", "en", split="train", streaming=True)
calib_texts = [next(iter(calib_data))["text"] for _ in range(512)]

def get_calibration_inputs(texts, tokenizer, seq_len=2048, n_samples=128):
    encodings = tokenizer("\n\n".join(texts), return_tensors="pt")
    input_ids = encodings.input_ids[:, : seq_len * n_samples]
    return input_ids.view(n_samples, seq_len).cuda()

calib_inputs = get_calibration_inputs(calib_texts, tokenizer)

The calibration dataset should represent the deployment distribution, not the training distribution. Using C4 for a medical model calibration can inflate quantization error significantly. Use domain-matched text even if unlabelled.
Extreme memory constraint; not recommended for general use

W8A8 means weights in INT8 and activations in INT8. W4A16 means weights in INT4 but activations remain in FP16/BF16. The latter is the most common deployment format because activation quantization to INT4 degrades quality significantly for LLMs with outlier activations.

The simplest PTQ approach — round-to-nearest (RTN) — applies the scale-and-round formula above independently to each weight tensor. For 8-bit quantization on moderate-sized models, RTN achieves near-FP16 quality. At 4 bits, RTN fails badly:

import torch

def rtn_quantize_weight(W: torch.Tensor, bits: int = 8) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, float]:
    """Round-to-nearest symmetric quantization (naive PTQ)."""
    max_int = 2 ** (bits - 1) - 1
    scale = W.abs().max() / max_int
    W_int = (W / scale).round().clamp(-max_int - 1, max_int).to(torch.int8)
    return W_int, scale

# For a typical 70B model with INT4 RTN:
# - Codebook range reduced to 16 discrete values (−8 … 7)
# - Outlier weights (common in LLMs) are catastrophically clipped
# - Perplexity on WikiText-2: FP16 ≈ 3.5 PPL; INT4 RTN ≈ 8–12 PPL (unacceptable)

The fundamental problem is that LLM weight tensors contain outliers — a small fraction of weights whose magnitudes are 10–100× larger than the median. When the scale is set by max(|W|), the majority of weights are crushed into only a few quantization levels.

GPTQ (Frantar et al., 2022) is the dominant PTQ method for 4-bit LLM quantization. It quantizes weights one column at a time, using the Hessian of the reconstruction error to compensate for the rounding error introduced at each step — a variant of the Optimal Brain Compression (OBC) framework.
For a linear layer with weight matrix W and calibration activations X, the reconstruction error is:

GPTQ minimises this error greedily, column by column, updating remaining columns after each quantization step to absorb the accumulated error. The per-column Hessian H = XX^T / |X| guides which directions in weight space to compensate:

# Install: pip install auto-gptq
from auto_gptq import AutoGPTQForCausalLM, BaseQuantizeConfig
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

quantize_config = BaseQuantizeConfig(
    bits=4,               # INT4
    group_size=128,       # independent scale per 128-weight group (better accuracy)
    desc_act=False,       # activation ordering — set True for slightly better accuracy
)

model = AutoGPTQForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3-8B",
    quantize_config=quantize_config,
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3-8B")

# Run calibration (128 samples, 2048 tokens each)
examples = [tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt") for text in calib_texts[:128]]
model.quantize(examples)

# Save GPTQ-quantized model
model.save_quantized("llama-3-8b-gptq-int4")
# Loads back with: AutoGPTQForCausalLM.from_quantized("llama-3-8b-gptq-int4", device="cuda")

Instead of computing a single scale per weight matrix (per-tensor quantization), group quantization divides each row of the weight matrix into groups of G consecutive elements and computes an independent scale for each group. This dramatically reduces quantization error for outlier-heavy weight tensors at the cost of slightly increased storage for scale factors:

Common group sizes: G = 128 (standard), G = 64 (higher accuracy, more overhead), G = 32 (near-lossless at INT4 for many tasks). At G = 128, the overhead is roughly 1 bit per parameter — making an effective 4-bit quantization closer to ~4.125 bits.
SmoothQuant: Solving the Activation Outlier Problem

Weight tensors can be quantized accurately with GPTQ. Activation tensors are harder: they contain extreme outliers at specific hidden dimensions (observed across all LLMs above 6.7B parameters), making INT8 activation quantization lossy. SmoothQuant (Xiao et al., 2022) migrates the quantization difficulty from activations to weights using a per-channel smoothing factor:
The scale s is chosen to balance the magnitude of activations and weights per channel. After smoothing, both X and W have comparable ranges and can be quantized to INT8 without significant error:

# Install: pip install smoothquant
from smoothquant.smooth import smooth_lm
from smoothquant.calibration import get_act_scales
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3-8B", torch_dtype=torch.float16).cuda()
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3-8B")

# Step 1: measure per-channel activation scales on calibration data
act_scales = get_act_scales(model, tokenizer, calib_texts, num_samples=128, seq_len=512)

# Step 2: fold smoothing factors into weights in-place (no retraining)
smooth_lm(model, act_scales, alpha=0.5)   # alpha=0.5 splits difficulty equally

# Step 3: quantize weights and activations to INT8
# model now ready for W8A8 inference via torch.ao.quantization or TensorRT

AWQ (Lin et al., 2023) takes a different approach: instead of smoothing activations, it identifies the 1% of weight channels that are most important for preserving model quality (those activated by the largest activation values) and protects them from quantization error by applying per-channel scaling before quantization:
For each weight output channel, compute the average activation magnitude over the calibration set. Channels with high activation magnitude have larger impact on model output.
For each salient channel, search for a scale factor s that minimises the layerwise quantization error ||WX - Q(W·diag(s))·diag(s)⁻¹·X||.
The scale factors are folded into the weight tensor offline, so inference is identical to standard INT4 matrix multiply.

# Install: pip install autoawq
from awq import AutoAWQForCausalLM
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

model = AutoAWQForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3-8B")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3-8B")

quant_config = {
    "zero_point": True,    # asymmetric quantization
    "q_group_size": 128,   # group size
    "w_bit": 4,            # 4-bit weights
    "version": "GEMM",     # CUDA kernel variant
}

model.quantize(tokenizer, quant_config=quant_config, calib_data=calib_texts)
model.save_quantized("llama-3-8b-awq", safetensors=True)

# Reload:
# model = AutoAWQForCausalLM.from_quantized("llama-3-8b-awq", fuse_layers=True)
When to Use Each PTQ Method
Good at INT8, poor at INT4
Server inference with INT8 Tensor Core acceleration

The standard metrics for PTQ evaluation are perplexity on a held-out text corpus and downstream task accuracy. Always evaluate both — perplexity can understate quality loss on structured tasks like coding and math:

import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_dataset
import math

def evaluate_perplexity(model_path: str, dataset_name: str = "wikitext", split: str = "test"):
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_path, torch_dtype=torch.float16).cuda()
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_path)
    model.eval()

    data = load_dataset(dataset_name, "wikitext-2-raw-v1", split=split)
    text = "\n\n".join(data["text"])
    encodings = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")

    seq_len = 2048
    total_nll, total_tokens = 0.0, 0

    for i in range(0, encodings.input_ids.size(1) - seq_len, seq_len):
        chunk = encodings.input_ids[:, i : i + seq_len].cuda()
        with torch.no_grad():
            output = model(chunk, labels=chunk)
        total_nll += output.loss.item() * seq_len
        total_tokens += seq_len

    ppl = math.exp(total_nll / total_tokens)
    print(f"{model_path}: PPL = {ppl:.2f}")
    return ppl

# Typical results for Llama-3 8B on WikiText-2:
# BF16 baseline:  PPL ≈ 6.14
# GPTQ INT4 g128: PPL ≈ 6.55  (+6.7%)
# AWQ  INT4 g128: PPL ≈ 6.48  (+5.5%)
# RTN  INT4:      PPL ≈ 8.91  (+45% — unacceptable)
Calibrate with samples from the deployment domain
Always use group quantization (g_size ≤ 128) at INT4
Quantizing embedding and LM head layers
Leave embedding and LM head in FP16 (standard practice)
W8A8 SmoothQuant skipped; INT8 accuracy poor
Apply SmoothQuant before INT8 activation quantization
PPL looks fine but coding/math performance tanks
Always run task evals (HumanEval, GSM8K) alongside PPL
Scale factors poorly estimated, high variance
Use ≥128 samples; 512 is safer for large models
When INT4 PTQ quality is insufficient

GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format) is the quantization format used by llama.cpp, the most widely used CPU inference engine for LLMs. Unlike GPTQ and AWQ which target GPU Tensor Cores, GGUF quantization is optimised for CPU SIMD instructions (AVX2, AVX-512, NEON on Apple Silicon) and can mix bit widths per layer:
Aggressive compression; significant quality loss above 13B
Uniform INT4 per block of 32; fast, moderate accuracy
Mixed 4/6-bit with K-quant method; strong accuracy/speed trade-off
Near-INT8 accuracy at 5.5 bits; recommended for quality-sensitive use
Very close to FP16 quality on most tasks

# Convert a Hugging Face model to GGUF with llama.cpp
git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp
cd llama.cpp && make -j$(nproc)

# Convert FP16 HF model to GGUF base format
python3 convert_hf_to_gguf.py /path/to/llama-3-8b --outfile llama-3-8b-f16.gguf

# Quantize to Q4_K_M (recommended balance of speed and accuracy)
./llama-quantize llama-3-8b-f16.gguf llama-3-8b-Q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M

# Run inference
./llama-cli -m llama-3-8b-Q4_K_M.gguf -p "Explain gradient descent:" -n 200 --threads 8

The K-quant methods (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q6_K) use a hybrid approach: attention key/value projection weights and feed-forward down-projection weights — which are empirically more sensitive — are quantized at higher precision, while the remaining weights use lower precision. This yields better accuracy than uniform quantization at the same average bit count.

Not all layers tolerate quantization equally. The first and last transformer layers and layers that process high-entropy tokens (e.g. punctuation, rare words) are more sensitive to quantization error than middle layers. Mixed-precision quantization assigns different bit widths per layer or per weight type to maximise accuracy within a target average bit budget:
Always kept in FP16/BF16. These layers operate on token indices and vocabulary logits where INT4 causes visible output degradation.
Often quantized to INT8 rather than INT4. These layers process and produce the most semantically dense representations.
Typically the largest layers by parameter count, and relatively robust to 4-bit quantization — good candidates for aggressive compression.
Moderately sensitive. Per-group quantization at G=64 or G=32 mitigates accuracy loss.

# Example: mixed-precision quantization config for AutoGPTQ
from auto_gptq import AutoGPTQForCausalLM, BaseQuantizeConfig

# Define per-layer quantization bit widths
# Format: {layer_name_pattern: bits}
quantize_config = BaseQuantizeConfig(
    bits=4,           # default for all layers
    group_size=128,
    desc_act=False,
    # Override specific layer types to higher precision
    # (auto-gptq allows layer-name-based overrides via model.quantize kwargs)
)

# For manual mixed-precision with HQQ or TorchAO:
from torchao.quantization import quantize_, int8_weight_only, int4_weight_only
from torchao.quantization.granularity import PerGroup

model = ...  # loaded FP16 model

# INT4 for MLP layers, INT8 for attention
for name, module in model.named_modules():
    if "mlp" in name and hasattr(module, "weight"):
        quantize_(module, int4_weight_only(group_size=128))
    elif "attn" in name and hasattr(module, "weight"):
        quantize_(module, int8_weight_only())
Real-World Example: Llama-3 70B on a Single A100 80GB
A full-precision Llama-3 70B model in BF16 requires approximately 140 GB VRAM — more than two A100 80GB GPUs. PTQ makes single-GPU deployment feasible:
Collect 256 domain-representative calibration samples (for a medical chatbot: clinical notes and medical Q&A pairs).
Apply AWQ W4A16 with group_size=128 — calibration takes ~12 minutes on a single A100.
The quantized model occupies ~37 GB VRAM, fitting on one A100 with ~43 GB headroom for KV cache.
Run the MMLU benchmark (medical subset) to verify quality: AWQ INT4 achieves 82.1% vs FP16 baseline of 83.8% — a 1.7% drop.
Deploy with vLLM using the AWQ backend for 4× throughput vs multi-GPU FP16 serving.

# Serve AWQ-quantized Llama-3 70B with vLLM
from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

llm = LLM(
    model="llama-3-70b-awq",
    quantization="awq",
    dtype="float16",
    max_model_len=8192,
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.90,
    # Single A100 80GB with 37 GB for weights + 43 GB for KV cache
)

sampling_params = SamplingParams(temperature=0.7, max_tokens=512)
outputs = llm.generate(["Summarize the key findings of this clinical trial:"], sampling_params)
print(outputs[0].outputs[0].text)

At INT4 W4A16, a 70B model running on a single A100 achieves roughly 35 tokens/sec — compared to ~10 tokens/sec for FP16 on 2× A100 with tensor parallelism. The quantized model is both cheaper and faster to serve.
Before shipping a quantized model to production, verify each of the following:
Perplexity delta vs FP16 baseline is < 5% on held-out domain text.
Downstream task accuracy (MMLU / GSM8K / HumanEval) is within 3% of FP16 on the tasks you care about.
Calibration data matches the deployment domain, not just generic web text.
Embedding and LM head layers are kept in FP16 (not quantized).
Group size ≤ 128 for INT4 quantization (never per-tensor at INT4).
Inference kernel is compatible with target hardware (exllama2 for GPTQ on NVIDIA, llama.cpp for CPU, vLLM AWQ backend for server inference).
KV cache size budget is recalculated for the new VRAM headroom (INT4 weights free up more VRAM for longer context).
Toxicity and safety benchmarks re-run — quantization can shift safety-relevant probability mass in edge cases.

Post-training quantization is the fastest path from a full-precision LLM to a deployable, memory-efficient model. The workflow is: (1) choose a target precision (INT8 for servers with Tensor Core acceleration, INT4 for consumer or on-device inference), (2) collect 128–512 calibration samples from the deployment domain, (3) apply the appropriate PTQ algorithm — SmoothQuant for W8A8, GPTQ or AWQ for INT4 W4A16, (4) verify perplexity and downstream task accuracy before deployment. Naive round-to-nearest at 4 bits is never acceptable for models above 1B parameters; always use Hessian-guided (GPTQ) or activation-aware (AWQ) methods at INT4.
